name: United States Senate
constraints: Cannot legislate without concurrence of the House of Representatives
Presidential veto requires two-thirds supermajority to override
Advice and consent role limited to appointments and treaties; cannot directly compel executive action
Senators serve staggered six-year terms, limiting rapid partisan realignment
factional_pressures: Clay Whigs seeking to legislate the American System through a compliant executive and punish Tyler's apostasy
Southern Whig senators torn between party loyalty and states'-rights sympathies awakened by Tyler's constitutional position
Democratic minority under Benton and Calhoun calculating how best to exploit the Whig civil war without appearing to aid a renegade
Anti-slavery voices in the Northern Whig caucus whose demands Clay must manage without fracturing his coalition
public_legitimacy: High among the propertied and educated classes who regard the Senate as the republic's deliberative conscience; viewed with suspicion by Jacksonian Democrats who see it as an aristocratic brake on popular will.
plausible_actions: Reject Tyler's cabinet nominees as a demonstration of senatorial supremacy and factional punishment
Pass Clay's national bank bill and dare Tyler to veto it, forcing a constitutional confrontation
Expunge or refuse to receive executive communications that assume full presidential authority rather than acting presidential capacity
Censure the President by resolution, as the Senate censured Jackson in 1834, to signal institutional displeasure without legal remedy
Delay or refuse confirmation of Tyler's judicial and diplomatic appointments to strangle his patronage capacity
implausible_actions: Impeach the President on the sole grounds of his constitutional interpretation of the succession clause, without specific high crimes alleged
Formally declare Tyler a mere acting president and seat a new executive through legislative fiat
Conduct business through secret sessions on matters of domestic legislation, which Senate rules and custom did not permit

name: United States House of Representatives
constraints: All revenue and appropriations bills must originate in the House, giving it fiscal leverage but requiring Senate concurrence to become law
Members serve two-year terms, making the body acutely sensitive to electoral currents and constituent opinion
Speaker's authority depends on maintaining a working majority that the Whig civil war renders increasingly fragile
Cannot act on treaties or executive appointments, limiting its direct check on Tyler's foreign and patronage policy
factional_pressures: Clay loyalists who regard the House as the engine of Whig legislative ambition and demand aggressive confrontation with Tyler
Moderate Whigs from competitive districts who fear that a prolonged constitutional crisis will cost them their seats in 1842
Democratic members under former President Van Buren's shadow, content to vote against Whig measures while watching the majority destroy itself
John Quincy Adams, returned to the House after his presidency, whose moral authority and anti-slavery convictions complicate simple party calculation
public_legitimacy: Regarded by Jacksonian Democrats as the most democratic branch, closest to the people; Whigs value it as the legislative instrument of national improvement but subordinate it rhetorically to senatorial deliberation.
plausible_actions: Pass Clay's bank bill and internal improvements legislation as a direct challenge to Tyler's veto power
Initiate impeachment inquiries through select committee investigation, though conviction remains implausible
Refuse to appropriate funds for executive branch operations Tyler favours, using the power of the purse as leverage
Pass resolutions condemning Tyler's vetoes as an unconstitutional usurpation of legislative authority
Invite public memorials and petitions against the President's conduct, amplifying popular pressure through formal procedure
implausible_actions: Convict and remove the President through impeachment without a Senate trial, acting as sole judge of executive fitness
Legislate a formal definition of presidential succession that retroactively alters Tyler's status without constitutional amendment
Compel the President to appear before the House to answer questions, after the fashion of parliamentary question time

name: The Whig Party
constraints: A coalition assembled from incompatible factions—National Republicans, Anti-Masons, Southern states'-righters, and conscience Whigs—held together by opposition to Jackson rather than affirmative doctrine
Has never governed before 1841 and possesses no established machinery for managing executive-legislative relations
Dependent on the mythology of Tippecanoe and the log-cabin campaign, which provided no governing mandate beyond vague promises of reform
Clay's dominance of the party's congressional wing creates a structural rivalry with any Whig executive who possesses independent will
factional_pressures: Clay's congressional Whigs who regard themselves as the true custodians of Whig principle and intend to govern through legislation regardless of Tyler
Webster's faction, more nationalist and less doctrinaire, willing to negotiate with Tyler to preserve cabinet influence and avoid total rupture
Southern Whigs who find Tyler's states'-rights constitutionalism more congenial than Clay's centralising programme and resist expulsion of the President
Northern evangelical and reform-minded Whigs whose anti-slavery and temperance convictions sit uneasily with the party's Southern wing
public_legitimacy: Enjoys the mandate of the 1840 landslide but that mandate was personal to Harrison and theatrical in character; the party's claim to govern rests on congressional majorities rather than a coherent popular philosophy.
plausible_actions: Formally expel Tyler from the Whig Party by caucus resolution, stripping him of partisan identity and patronage legitimacy
Orchestrate the mass resignation of the cabinet—save Webster—to demonstrate that Tyler governs without a party
Nominate Clay for the presidency in 1844 and construct the entire 1841-42 legislative programme as a platform for that campaign
Use the partisan press to wage a sustained reputational war against Tyler, branding him a traitor and usurper
Recruit and fund Whig primary challengers against any member of Congress who votes with Tyler or accepts his patronage
implausible_actions: Formally merge with the Democratic Party to create a unified anti-Tyler coalition, given the depth of partisan and ideological antagonism
Abandon the national bank as a party objective in order to reach accommodation with Tyler on other matters
Accept Tyler's constitutional interpretation of succession as legitimate party doctrine

name: The Democratic Party
constraints: Ejected from the executive branch after twelve years of dominance, the party lacks patronage and must rebuild from congressional minority status
The legacy of the Panic of 1837 clings to the Van Buren administration and undermines Democratic credibility on economic management
Calhoun's faction threatens to splinter the Southern wing if the party does not adopt a more aggressive states'-rights and pro-slavery posture
Van Buren's shadow over the party creates a factional rivalry between his loyalists and those who regard him as an electoral liability
factional_pressures: Van Buren loyalists who regard 1840 as a temporary defeat and expect to reclaim the presidency in 1844 by maintaining party discipline
Calhoun's South Carolina nullifiers who view Tyler's accession as an opportunity to detach the South from Whig allegiance and reconstruct a Southern-dominated Democracy
Western Democrats under Thomas Hart Benton who distrust both Calhoun's extremism and Van Buren's eastern establishment instincts
Working-class urban Democrats in Northern cities whose hostility to banks aligns with Tyler's veto of the national bank but whose other loyalties remain firmly Democratic
public_legitimacy: Still commands the loyalty of a substantial popular majority in the South and among urban labourers in the North; the 1840 defeat is understood as a defeat of personality and theatre, not of Jacksonian principle.
plausible_actions: Vote with Tyler against the national bank bill, accepting the enemy of their enemy as a temporary instrument without formal alliance
Position Van Buren as the inevitable 1844 nominee and use every Tyler veto as evidence that Whig governance is inherently chaotic
Cultivate Calhoun's ambitions carefully, neither endorsing nor alienating him, while preventing his faction from capturing the party
Use the partisan Democratic press to amplify Tyler's constitutional arguments while simultaneously mocking Whig disarray
Obstruct Whig legislation in the Senate through procedural delay, forcing the majority to exhaust itself on internal quarrels
implausible_actions: Formally invite Tyler to join the Democratic Party and endorse him as their 1844 presidential candidate
Support Clay's national bank bill as a pragmatic concession to economic stability
Abandon opposition to internal improvements funded by federal revenue, a position central to Jacksonian orthodoxy

name: The Presidential Cabinet
constraints: Cabinet members serve at the pleasure of the President; Tyler may dismiss any of them, and they have no constitutional standing independent of executive will
The Whig theory of cabinet government—that the cabinet should govern collectively and constrain the President—has no basis in American constitutional law or precedent
Webster's decision to remain after the mass resignation leaves the cabinet fractured and its collective authority destroyed
Cabinet members confirmed by a hostile Senate may find their departmental budgets and subordinate appointments obstructed as proxy punishment
factional_pressures: Clay's loyalists within the original Harrison cabinet who regard themselves as the true Whig government and attempt to constrain Tyler through collective pressure
Webster, whose diplomatic ambitions and nationalist convictions make him willing to serve Tyler while the others resign, creating a faction of one
Tyler's subsequent nominees, Southern Democrats and conservative Whigs, who face Senate rejection and leave departments in administrative limbo
The ghost of Harrison's cabinet arrangements, under which Tyler was reportedly expected to defer to collective cabinet judgment—an expectation Tyler flatly rejects from the first meeting
public_legitimacy: Poorly understood by the general public, whose conception of executive government is personalised in the President rather than distributed among ministers; the mass resignation is received as a political spectacle rather than a constitutional statement.
plausible_actions: Present Tyler with a collective memorandum demanding he submit major decisions to cabinet vote before acting—and resign en masse when he refuses
Leak cabinet deliberations to the partisan Whig press to build public pressure against Tyler's independent course
Webster negotiate secretly with Tyler to preserve his position as Secretary of State while his colleagues resign, trading loyalty for influence over foreign policy
Tyler's new cabinet nominees publicly articulate a theory of executive supremacy that directly contradicts the Whig governing philosophy
Individual cabinet members accept Tyler's patronage authority and quietly distance themselves from Clay's congressional programme
implausible_actions: The cabinet vote to collectively override a presidential decision and implement policy contrary to Tyler's explicit instruction
Cabinet members appeal to Congress to legislate their collective authority over the executive, establishing a parliamentary-style council by statute
The cabinet publicly endorse Clay's presidential candidacy for 1844 while still serving Tyler

name: The Partisan Press
constraints: Newspapers are financially dependent on party patronage—government printing contracts and subscription networks organised through party machinery—making editorial independence structurally difficult
The speed of information is limited to horse, rail, and the nascent telegraph; Washington news reaches New York in hours but the frontier in days or weeks
Libel law is inconsistently enforced and rarely deployed against political commentary, but social retaliation and duelling culture impose informal constraints on the most extreme personal attacks
Editors must balance the demands of their party patrons against the commercial need to attract readers across factional lines in competitive urban markets
factional_pressures: Whig organs such as the National Intelligencer, which must navigate between Clay's aggressive line and the more moderate Webster faction without losing either audience
Democratic papers that find themselves in the paradoxical position of defending Tyler's constitutional arguments while attacking his political character
The emerging penny press, less dependent on party subsidy and more dependent on sensation, which treats the succession crisis as entertainment as much as politics
Southern papers whose states'-rights sympathies make Tyler's constitutional position genuinely attractive, regardless of party label
public_legitimacy: Readers understand that newspapers are partisan instruments and discount accordingly; yet the press remains the primary mechanism by which political argument reaches the public, and a story repeated across multiple organs acquires the force of established fact.
plausible_actions: Wage a sustained campaign branding Tyler 'His Accidency' to delegitimise his claim to full presidential authority
Publish leaked cabinet memoranda, congressional correspondence, and diplomatic dispatches to shape public interpretation of the constitutional crisis
Organise public opinion through editorials calling for mass petitions to Congress demanding Tyler's removal or submission
Elevate Clay as the true Whig leader and frame every legislative confrontation as Clay's righteous battle against executive usurpation
Circulate rumours of Tyler's alleged monarchical ambitions, drinking habits, and personal corruption to erode his public standing
implausible_actions: Maintain editorial neutrality on the succession question, treating it as a purely legal matter above partisan interpretation
Coordinate across Whig and Democratic papers to present a unified constitutional position on presidential succession
Conduct anything resembling systematic investigative journalism in the modern sense, with verification standards and source protection protocols

name: The Washington Social Circuit
constraints: Operates entirely through informal custom and personal relationship; possesses no legal authority and cannot compel attendance or compliance
Social exclusion is a powerful sanction but a slow one; a politician willing to endure ostracism from drawing rooms can survive if he retains electoral support
The circuit is geographically concentrated in a small city where everyone knows everyone, making genuine secrecy nearly impossible and rumour nearly instantaneous

Women, who manage the drawing rooms and dinner tables that constitute the circuit's primary venues, exercise real political influence through access and exclusion but cannot translate it into formal institutional power
factional_pressures: Clay's social circle, centred on his lodgings and the homes of his Senate allies, which functions as an informal war council and intelligence exchange
The Tyler White House, which must construct a new social network from scratch after the Whig establishment withdraws its invitations
Webster's salon, which occupies a peculiar middle position—maintaining contact with both Clay's circle and the executive—and thereby becomes a site of genuine intelligence brokerage

The diplomatic community, whose dinner tables are nominally neutral ground but whose sympathies and reports to their home governments constitute a secondary political audience for every Washington drama
public_legitimacy: Not a public institution and makes no claim to legitimacy; its authority is entirely tacit, resting on the shared understanding among Washington's political class that access to the right tables is inseparable from political survival.
plausible_actions: Socially ostracise Tyler and his family from Whig drawing rooms, signalling to wavering politicians the cost of association with the President
Use dinner table intelligence to identify which senators might be persuaded to break with Clay on specific votes and target them for private lobbying
Orchestrate public social humiliations—snubs, uninvitations, the withdrawal of calling cards—that translate into newspaper items and political signals
Provide Tyler's enemies with the personal information—debts, domestic difficulties, past statements—needed to construct reputational attacks
Allow the diplomatic corps to observe and report on the Whig civil war, shaping European perceptions of American governmental stability
implausible_actions: Formally organise as a political club or association with membership rolls, rules, and collective resolutions
Extend full social equality to free Black Washingtonians or permit their participation in the political intelligence network on equal terms
Operate with sufficient discretion to prevent the partisan press from learning and publishing the substance of private dinner-table conversations

name: The United States Army
constraints: The regular army numbers fewer than twelve thousand men, scattered across frontier posts and coastal fortifications, with no capacity for domestic political intervention
Military appropriations are controlled by Congress, making the army institutionally dependent on the legislative branch and structurally incapable of independent political action
The tradition of civilian supremacy over the military is sufficiently established that any general who involved himself in the succession dispute would face immediate and universal condemnation
The officer corps is divided between West Point professionals and politically appointed officers whose loyalties follow their patrons rather than institutional command
factional_pressures: Officers whose commissions and promotions depend on executive patronage and who therefore have a material interest in the outcome of the succession question
The frontier army's practical irrelevance to Washington politics creates a corps of officers who regard the capital's constitutional drama with professional detachment
Democratic officers who served under Jackson and regard the Whig seizure of the executive with personal hostility
Scott and other ambitious generals who understand that a prolonged political crisis may create opportunities for military men to acquire political prominence
public_legitimacy: The army is regarded with a mixture of frontier practicality and republican suspicion; Americans honour military heroes but distrust standing armies as instruments of tyranny—a tension Harrison's campaign exploited and Tyler cannot.
plausible_actions: Remain entirely passive in the constitutional crisis, executing lawful orders from whoever occupies the executive office without adjudicating the succession question
Provide Tyler with the ceremonial apparatus of presidential authority—salutes, guards, formal military courtesies—that reinforces his claim to full executive status
Allow ambitious officers to seek political office through the established custom of resignation from service and entry into partisan politics
Continue frontier operations against Native nations under existing congressional authorisations, independent of the political crisis in Washington
Winfield Scott position himself as a potential Whig presidential alternative to Clay, using military reputation as political capital
implausible_actions: Intervene militarily to resolve the succession dispute or enforce a congressional interpretation of Tyler's constitutional status
Refuse to obey Tyler's orders on the grounds that he is only an acting president without full executive authority
Conduct a coup or threaten one as a mechanism for resolving political deadlock, in a republic with no tradition of military governance
